original_hypothesis.title: Unclearable Bad Debt Causes Infinite Interest Accrual
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In liquidate(), if a user's position is heavily underwater, liquidating their full debt requires more collateral than they possess, causing an underflow revert. Liquidators must partially liquidate until collateral reaches zero. The remaining bad debt cannot be cleared because any further liquidation attempts will revert when subtracting from zero collateral.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This permanently corrupts totalBorrow accounting, causing totalBorrow.elastic to grow infinitely due to interest on uncollectible bad debt, diluting the debt shares of all healthy borrowers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Underflow revert when required collateralShare exceeds userCollateralShare

preconditions: A user position becomes heavily underwater (collateral value < borrow value * liquidation multiplier)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of bad debt write-off mechanism
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Capping collateralShare to available collateral and writing off remaining debt

analysis_questions: What happens when a user has 0 collateral but > 0 debt?
Does interest continue to accrue on this debt?
validation_requirements: Attempt to liquidate the remaining debt and verify it reverts
Verify interest continues to accrue on the bad debt

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Market crash causes a position to become heavily underwater. Liquidators strip all collateral, leaving bad debt. The bad debt remains in totalBorrow.base forever, accruing interest and inflating totalBorrow.elastic.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Corruption of global debt accounting, unfair interest burden on healthy borrowers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires admin intervention or contract upgrade

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for liquidating heavily underwater positions

validation_result.mechanism: Missing bad debt write-off in liquidation
validation_result.impact_summary: When a position becomes heavily underwater, collateral can be fully stripped but remaining debt cannot be cleared, causing perpetual interest accrual on uncollectable bad debt that dilutes all healthy borrowers.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The liquidate() function in CauldronV4.sol computes collateralShare from borrowAmount using the LIQUIDATION_MULTIPLIER and exchangeRate. When a position is heavily underwater, this computed collateralShare can exceed the user's actual userCollateralShare. The BoringMath .sub() operation reverts on underflow, so liquidators must reduce maxBorrowParts to avoid overflow. This means they can only liquidate debt proportional to available collateral. Once collateral reaches zero but debt remains, any further liquidation attempt computes collateralShare > 0 from the remaining borrowAmount, but userCollateralShare[user] is 0, causing an underflow revert. The remaining bad debt stays in totalBorrow permanently. Since accrue() applies interest to the entire totalBorrow.elastic, this bad debt accumulates interest forever, inflating the elastic-to-base ratio and effectively increasing all healthy borrowers' actual debt burden. There is no admin function or protocol mechanism to write off or socialize this bad debt.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible in the liquidate() function where collateralShare is computed purely from borrowAmount without being capped to userCollateralShare[user]. The CauldronLib.sol helper function getLiquidationCollateralAndBorrowAmount (lines ~107-133) actually contains an off-chain workaround that caps borrowPart to maxBorrowPart based on available collateral, proving the developers were aware of this edge case. However, the on-chain liquidate() function itself lacks this cap. The accrue() function unconditionally adds interest to totalBorrow.elastic regardless of whether the underlying debt is collectible.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: collateralShare is computed from borrowAmount without being capped to userCollateralShare[user]. When collateral is worth less than debt * LIQUIDATION_MULTIPLIER, the computed collateralShare exceeds available collateral. When collateral is zero, any non-zero borrowPart produces non-zero collateralShare, causing underflow revert on the .sub() call.

Interest accrues on the entire totalBorrow.elastic, which includes uncollectable bad debt. Since bad debt cannot be removed from totalBorrow, interest compounds on it indefinitely, inflating the elastic-to-base ratio and increasing the effective debt of all healthy borrowers.

calling_sequence: 1. Market crash causes user position to become heavily underwater (collateral value << debt * LIQUIDATION_MULTIPLIER)
2. Liquidator calls liquidate() with maxBorrowParts calculated to strip all remaining collateral
3. collateralShare is computed from borrowAmount but capped by what liquidator specified via borrowPart
4. After multiple partial liquidations, userCollateralShare[user] reaches 0 while userBorrowPart[user] > 0
5. Any subsequent liquidate() call: _isSolvent returns false (borrowPart>0, collateral==0), but collateralShare computed from any borrowPart > 0 is also > 0
7. Bad debt remains in totalBorrow permanently, accruing interest via accrue()
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path starts at liquidate() where for each insolvent user, collateralShare is derived from borrowAmount (line ~407-413). This collateralShare is subtracted from userCollateralShare[user] (line ~416) using BoringMath.sub which reverts on underflow. When collateral is zero, this always reverts for any non-zero borrowPart. Meanwhile, accrue() (line ~143-157) continues adding interest to totalBorrow.elastic which includes the stuck bad debt.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. It's an inherent code logic issue.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an attack per se but a natural consequence of market conditions. When collateral value drops sufficiently: (1) Liquidators strip collateral via partial liquidations using CauldronLib.getLiquidationCollateralAndBorrowAmount to compute safe maxBorrowParts. (2) Once collateral is zero, remaining debt is permanently stuck. (3) accrue() continues charging interest on the bad debt. (4) The totalBorrow.elastic/base ratio increases, meaning when healthy borrowers repay their borrowPart, they owe more in elastic terms than they originally borrowed (beyond normal interest). The bad debt interest effectively becomes a hidden tax on all borrowers.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH impact. All borrowers in the Cauldron are affected because interest on bad debt inflates totalBorrow.elastic, increasing the actual MIM amount each borrowPart represents. Over time, this creates a growing discrepancy between what healthy borrowers owe and what they originally borrowed. Protocol fees (feesEarned) also inflate as they accrue on uncollectable debt. Recovery requires admin intervention (e.g., contract upgrade or external debt repayment) since no on-chain mechanism exists to write off bad debt.
recommended_mitigations: Cap collateralShare to min(computedCollateralShare, userCollateralShare[user]) in liquidate(), and write off the remaining uncovered debt by reducing totalBorrow without requiring MIM payment
Add an admin function to socialize bad debt by reducing totalBorrow.elastic and totalBorrow.base for uncollectable positions
Implement a bad debt insurance fund or reserve system that automatically covers bad debt from protocol fees
Consider adding a keeper mechanism that detects and clears zero-collateral positions
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy CauldronV4 with a collateral token and oracle. 2. Create a position with collateral and borrow MIM. 3. Manipulate oracle to make position heavily underwater (collateral value < debt * LIQUIDATION_MULTIPLIER). 4. Liquidate repeatedly with carefully computed maxBorrowParts (using CauldronLib helper) until collateral reaches 0. 5. Verify userBorrowPart[user] > 0 and userCollateralShare[user] == 0. 6. Attempt to liquidate remaining debt and confirm revert. 7. Call accrue() after some time and verify totalBorrow.elastic increased due to interest on the bad debt. 8. Verify a healthy borrower's effective debt has increased.
uncertainties: The exact frequency and magnitude of bad debt events depends on market conditions and oracle latency
Someone could voluntarily call repay() to clear bad debt positions, though there is no economic incentive to do so
Admin could potentially upgrade the contract via a new masterContract to add bad debt handling
additional_testing_needed: Quantify the rate of totalBorrow.elastic inflation from a single bad debt position over various time periods
Test edge case where multiple users simultaneously have bad debt
Verify that CauldronLib.getLiquidationCollateralAndBorrowAmount correctly computes the maximum liquidatable amount to strip all collateral without reverting
Test whether the bad debt interest eventually causes totalBorrow.elastic overflow
